title: Graticule (cartography)

infobox.value: or **grid** (from [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language")  **[crāticula](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/craticula#Latin "wikt:craticula")** 'grill/grating'), on a [map](/wiki/Map "Map"), is a graphical depiction of a [coordinate system](/wiki/Spatial_reference_system "Spatial reference system") as a [grid](/wiki/Projected_coordinate_system "Projected coordinate system") of [coordinate curves](/wiki/Coordinate_curve "Coordinate curve") or "lines", each curve/line representing a constant [coordinate](/wiki/Coordinate_system "Coordinate system") value.[[1]](#cite_note-Robinson1995-1) It is thus a form of [isoline](/wiki/Contour_line "Contour line"), and is commonly found on maps of many kinds, at scales from local to global.
Robinson, Arthur H.; Morrison, Joel L.; Muehrcke, Phillip C.; Kimerling, A. Jon; Guptill, Stephen C. (1995). *Elements of Cartography* (6th ed.). Wiley. p. 48.
Dent, Borden D., Jeffrey S. Torguson, Thomas W. Hodler, *Cartography: Thematic Map Design*, 6th Edition, McGraw-Hill, 2009, p.27
Kimerling, A. Jon; Buckley, Aileen R.; Muehrcke, Philip C.; Muehrcke, Juliana O. (2012). *Map Use: Reading, Analysis, Interpretation* (7th ed.). Esri Press. pp. 13–16.
Slocum, Terry A., Robert B. McMaster, Fritz C. Kessler, Hugh H. Howard, *Thematic Cartography and Visualization*, 3rd Edition, Pearson-Prentice Hall, 2009, p.201

Strabo (1917). Jones, Horace Leonard (ed.). *Geographica*. Vol. 2. Harvard University Press. p. 253. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.4159/DLCL.strabo-geography.1917](https://doi.org/10.4159%2FDLCL.strabo-geography.1917).
Cordell, D.K. Yee (1994). "Taking the World's Measure: Chinese Maps between Observation and Text". In Harley, J.B.; Woodward, David (eds.). *The History of Cartography*. University of Chicago Press.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Grid on a map, depicting a coordinate system

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Europe_orthographic_Caucasus_Urals_boundary_%28with_borders%29.svg/330px-Europe_orthographic_Caucasus_Urals_boundary_%28with_borders%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Europe_orthographic_Caucasus_Urals_boundary_(with_borders).svg)

Map of Europe with a 30° graticule in dark gray

A **graticule** or **grid** (from [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language")  **[crāticula](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/craticula#Latin "wikt:craticula")** 'grill/grating'), on a [map](/wiki/Map "Map"), is a graphical depiction of a [coordinate system](/wiki/Spatial_reference_system "Spatial reference system") as a [grid](/wiki/Projected_coordinate_system "Projected coordinate system") of [coordinate curves](/wiki/Coordinate_curve "Coordinate curve") or "lines", each curve/line representing a constant [coordinate](/wiki/Coordinate_system "Coordinate system") value.[[1]](#cite_note-Robinson1995-1) It is thus a form of [isoline](/wiki/Contour_line "Contour line"), and is commonly found on maps of many kinds, at scales from local to global.

The term *graticule* is almost always used to specifically refer to the [parallels](/wiki/Parallel_(latitude) "Parallel (latitude)") and [meridians](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)") of [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") and [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude"), respectively. In modern usage, graticules are contrasted with *grids*, which display the [eastings and northings](/wiki/Projected_coordinate_system "Projected coordinate system") of a [projected](/wiki/Map_projection "Map projection") [coordinate reference system](/wiki/Coordinate_reference_system "Coordinate reference system"), such as [Universal Transverse Mercator](/wiki/Universal_Transverse_Mercator "Universal Transverse Mercator") – usually the coordinate system in which the map is drawn.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Some cartographers have used the term "graticule" to refer not only to the visual lines, but to the system of latitude and longitude reference itself;[[3]](#cite_note-torguson-3) however, in the era of [geographic information systems](/wiki/Geographic_information_system "Geographic information system"), it is more common to call this the [geographic coordinate system](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system").

## Uses and design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Graticule_(cartography)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Uses and design")]

The graticule may serve several purposes on a map:[[4]](#cite_note-Kimerling-4)

* Aid map users in estimating the coordinates of locations
* Aid map users in identifying locations with known coordinates
* Indicate the [cardinal directions](/wiki/Cardinal_direction "Cardinal direction"), especially on map projections in which these directions vary across the map (e.g. [conic](/wiki/Conic_projection "Conic projection"), [pseudocylindrical](/wiki/Pseudocylindrical_projection "Pseudocylindrical projection"), [azimuthal](/wiki/Azimuthal_projection "Azimuthal projection")) where a north arrow or [compass rose](/wiki/Compass_rose "Compass rose") would not be appropriate[[5]](#cite_note-slocum-5)

These are usually secondary to the main purpose of the map, so graticules are often drawn to be relatively low in the [visual hierarchy](/wiki/Visual_hierarchy "Visual hierarchy").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Graticule_(cartography)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/1482_Ulm_Ptolemy_World_Map.jpg/330px-1482_Ulm_Ptolemy_World_Map.jpg)](/wiki/File:1482_Ulm_Ptolemy_World_Map.jpg)

1482 reproduction of the world map in the *[Geography](/wiki/Geography_(Ptolemy) "Geography (Ptolemy)")* of [Claudius Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy")

The graticule is of ancient origin, being almost as old as the concept of the [spherical Earth](/wiki/Spheroid "Spheroid"), [coordinate system](/wiki/Spatial_reference_system "Spatial reference system") for measuring geographic locations, and the [map projection](/wiki/Map_projection "Map projection"). [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo"), in his *[Geography](/wiki/Geographica "Geographica")* (ca 20 AD), states that the maps in [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes")'s *Geography* Book 3 (3rd century BC, now lost) contained lines "drawn from west to east, parallel to the equatorial line" (thus the term [parallel](/wiki/Parallel_(latitude) "Parallel (latitude)"))[[6]](#cite_note-6) [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy")'s *[Geography](/wiki/Geography_(Ptolemy) "Geography (Ptolemy)")* (ca 150 AD) gives detailed instructions for drawing the [parallels](/wiki/Parallel_(latitude) "Parallel (latitude)") and [meridians](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)") for his two [projections](/wiki/Map_projection "Map projection").[[7]](#cite_note-ptolemy-7)

The works of Ptolemy and other classical geographers were available to the scientists of [medieval Islam](/wiki/Geography_and_cartography_in_medieval_Islam "Geography and cartography in medieval Islam"). Some, such as [al-Khwarizmi](/wiki/Muhammad_ibn_Musa_al-Khwarizmi "Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi"), further developed these works, including creating maps on a graticule of latitude and longitude.

During the European [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), graticules disappeared from the few maps that were produced; [T and O maps](/wiki/T_and_O_map "T and O map") in particular were more concerned with religious [cosmology](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology") than accurate representation of location. The [portolan charts](/wiki/Portolan_chart "Portolan chart") of the 13th to 15th centuries were much more accurate, but used [rhumb lines](/wiki/Rhumb_line "Rhumb line") that were much more useful for sea navigation than latitude and longitude. At the same time, however, the rediscovery of Ptolemy and other classical knowledge of the shape and size of the Earth led to the recreation of some of the ancient maps with their graticules; the earliest extant copies of Ptolemy's *Geography* with his maps date to the 14th and 15th centuries.[[7]](#cite_note-ptolemy-7) Starting in the 16th century, the graticule has been ubiquitous on global and continental scale maps.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Song_Dynasty_Map.JPG/250px-Song_Dynasty_Map.JPG)](/wiki/File:Song_Dynasty_Map.JPG)

The Song-dynasty (12th-century) *Yu Ji Tu* map, based on a grid with a 100 *li* (about 30 mile) spacing.

There is some debate over whether the Chinese and other Asians knew the world to be spherical prior to Western contact, but most maps appear to assume regions as flat.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Although Chinese maps do not portray any concept of latitude and longitude, cartesian grids appear on some maps dating back to the 11th century.
